title: auth — tenancy hooks
description: Auth registers ONE tenancy hook — TenantErased triggers a listener that revokes every active session for every User under that tenant. Auth is otherwise TENANT-NEUTRAL by design: its tables reference identity_id / tokenable polymorphically, not tenant_id, so tenant provisioning does not require any auth-specific setup.

hooks.purpose: On tenant erasure: enumerate every User row where tenant_id = erased_tenant_id, revoke every active PAT + refresh token for those Users. Fires AllSessionsRevoked with tenant_id set. Idempotent — a retried tenant-erasure that has already revoked sessions is a no-op.
hooks.notes: This is the ONLY auth-flow tenancy hook. TenantProvisioned does NOT need a hook — auth's tables are polymorphic + identity-scoped, so a fresh tenant with zero Users has zero auth rows to seed.

rationale: Tenancy hooks are for state that must materialise on tenant provisioning (default rows, seed data, per-tenant tables). Auth doesn't have per-tenant tables — refresh tokens / password resets / MFA challenges / cross-app grants are all identity-scoped, and JWT signing keys are per-Application (not per-Tenant).

TenantErased DOES need a hook because active sessions issued to Users under that tenant must be revoked as part of the cascade. Without the hook, a User's PAT would remain valid until natural expiry — which is a security bug when the tenant is erased for compliance reasons.

The `RevokeAllSessionsForTenantListener` is idempotent, transactional, and fires AllSessionsRevoked once with the aggregated count — not per-PAT SessionRevoked events (which would flood the notifications queue).
